replacement_html: The "quarter-wave doesn’t apply at planetary scale" escape is closed in advance (preemptive). The strongest available defense against the pole-floor argument is to say f = c/(4H) is a parallel-plate simplification that breaks down at planetary scale, and the real Schumann mechanism is a transverse (TM) mode running around the cavity’s horizontal dimension: fn = (c / 2πR) × √(n(n+1)) ≈ 10.59 Hz for n = 1 on a 6,371 km sphere (Schumann 1952; formalized in Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002), with the observed 7.83 Hz produced by ionospheric damping. This is correct globe physics — and it cannot rescue the dome for four compounding reasons. (i) The dome is already on record using quarter-wave, and WIN-029’s numerical match vanishes if the dome retreats. The model page (V50.6, “Schumann Cavity Height”) writes H = c / (4 × f_Schumann) = 299,792 / (4 × 7.83) = 9,572 km, and WIN-029 is the credit claim for that exact inversion. A retreat to transverse modes does not save WIN-038 in isolation; it requires the author to simultaneously withdraw WIN-029 and delete the 9,572 km firmament-height figure propagated throughout the model. And the retreat is not free: WIN-029’s “confirmed” status rests specifically on the 5.3% agreement between the 9,572 km back-derivation and the model’s 9,086 km parameterization. That quantitative match is the entire reason WIN-029 is scored as a confirmation rather than a tautological unit conversion — and a retreat from quarter-wave erases the 5.3% agreement entirely, because the transverse-mode formula has no cavity-height output at all. WIN-029 stripped of the 9,572 km number collapses from “confirmation of a 9,086 km firmament” to the qualitative restatement “a conductive cavity exists,” which every globe textbook already grants. You cannot have quarter-wave for the back-derivation you want and transverse-mode for the forward-derivation you need. (ii) Transverse mode imports a globe fingerprint. The spherical transverse formula reproduces 7.83 Hz only when the wavelength-setting length equals Earth’s circumference, 2πREarth = 40,030 km using the mean-radius R = 6,371 km (the WGS84 equatorial circumference is 40,075 km and is the figure quoted in STEELMAN-03; the AE-projection identity disc_radius = π × R holds under either choice). The dome’s published disc diameter is 2 × 20,015 = 40,030 km — identical to four significant figures, because disc_radius = π × REarth is exactly the azimuthal-equidistant projection identity. Plugging dome disc diameter into the globe’s transverse formula is the classic tautology flagged in Part 6 (STEELMAN-03: Tesla Disc Diameter Lock): a number built from globe geometry by construction cannot independently confirm dome geometry. (iii) The dome’s non-uniform H(r) violates the transverse-mode boundary conditions. The fn ≈ 10.59 Hz formula assumes a closed, approximately uniform spherical shell; the dome’s H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r / 8,619) km varies by roughly a factor of ten across the disc (8,537 km at the pole down to ≈836 km at the 20,015 km rim) and terminates at an open rim. Borrowing the sphere’s mode formula without the sphere’s geometry is precisely the silent formula-switch already documented in Section 2.1. (iv) The natural disc-cavity escape — cylindrical-waveguide Bessel modes — also fails. A sophisticated defender may object that the honest transverse analog for a disc-with-ceiling is neither vertical quarter-wave nor spherical TM but a cylindrical-waveguide mode governed by Bessel-function zeros: f = jn,m · c / (2πa). For the dome’s disc of radius a = 20,015 km, the leading cutoffs are TM01 at j0,1 · c / (2πa) = 2.4048 · 299,792 / (2π · 20,015) ≈ 5.73 Hz, TM11 at ≈ 9.13 Hz, and TE11 at ≈ 4.39 Hz (Bessel zeros: DLMF §10.21; cylindrical-waveguide formula: Collin, Field Theory of Guided Waves, 2nd ed., Ch. 5). None match 7.83 Hz from dome geometry alone, and the one closest to it (TM11 at 9.13 Hz) requires the same a = 20,015 km that is π · REarth by the AE-projection identity — the globe fingerprint is imported a second time. (A defender may reply that the honest calculation is a coupled radial-vertical eigenvalue problem on the dome’s specific H(r), not the flat cylinder. Granted — but that problem still takes a = 20,015 km = π · REarth as its radial boundary, so the globe-fingerprint is imported regardless of the detailed spectrum; and Bessel-zero monotonicity plus domain containment (the dome cavity is strictly contained in the flat cylinder with H = 8,537 km) put the dome’s fundamental TM-like mode at ≥5.73 Hz as a lower bound. The dome has not computed or published any coupled-eigenvalue result that lands at 7.83 Hz.) There is no mode formula the dome has published, nor any it could borrow from globe physics or cylindrical-waveguide theory, that reaches 7.83 Hz from dome geometry alone: either the pole-floor holds (quarter-wave), or the globe is assumed (spherical transverse), or the disc-cavity Bessel modes miss by 20–45% while still requiring globe-circumference geometry to even be written down.

EXP-204 closed this escape in its anticipated_objections[0] (monotonicity + domain containment), but that response lived only in the EXP file, not in the paragraph a reader sees. A physics-sophisticated dome defender reading sub-argument (iv) alone would see the objection ("that’s a flat-cylinder approximation; my cavity has a curved upper boundary H(r), so the honest calculation is a coupled eigenvalue problem") un-answered. The parenthetical promotes the EXP-204 reply into the paragraph text, so the escape is closed in-section.

new_evidence_added: Explicit in-section statement of the domain-containment bound: the dome cavity {(r, z) : 0 ≤ r ≤ 20,015 km, 0 ≤ z ≤ H(r)} with H(r) = 8,537 · exp(−r/8,619) is strictly contained in the flat cylinder {0 ≤ r ≤ 20,015, 0 ≤ z ≤ 8,537}, so by Courant–Faber–Krahn monotonicity of Dirichlet eigenvalues under domain inclusion, the fundamental TM-like mode of the dome cavity is ≥ the flat-cylinder TM₀₁ cutoff = 5.73 Hz.

Explicit in-section statement that the radial boundary of the coupled problem is a = 20,015 km = π · R_Earth (AE-projection identity), so the globe fingerprint is imported via the domain itself, independent of the detailed H(r)-modulated spectrum.

anticipated_objections.objection: Domain containment plus Dirichlet monotonicity gives a lower bound on EIGENVALUES (and therefore on the fundamental TM mode). But Schumann is not a Dirichlet cavity; it is a Neumann-ish problem with mixed boundary conditions (PEC ground, imperfect upper conductor, open rim). The monotonicity argument therefore does not straightforwardly transfer.
anticipated_objections.response: The TM modes of a cylindrical cavity with perfect-electric-conductor (PEC) top and bottom and PEC side wall satisfy a Dirichlet problem for the scalar potential E_z (E_z = 0 on the side wall, ∂E_z/∂z = 0 on top/bottom for TM_n,m,0 modes) — the cutoff j_n,m · c / (2πa) is set entirely by the radial Dirichlet condition at r = a. Changing the top boundary shape from flat z = H to curved z = H(r) modifies the axial-mode structure but does not relax the radial Dirichlet condition, and if anything shrinking the cavity under that side-wall condition raises eigenvalues. The objection is correct that the vertical boundary conditions differ from a pure Dirichlet box, but the radial cutoff is what the lower bound rests on, and that cutoff is robust to top-boundary perturbations. A defender would need to POSIT a relaxed side-wall condition to drop below 5.73 Hz — and the dome has not published such a relaxation.

anticipated_objections.objection: The coupled problem might admit a low-frequency mode analogous to a Helmholtz resonator (a mode that is not well-approximated by the cutoff of any waveguide family) which could in principle sit near 7.83 Hz.
anticipated_objections.response: Helmholtz resonance requires a bulk air-cavity coupled through a narrow neck to an ambient reservoir — a geometry the dome explicitly does not have (the cavity is closed or nearly-closed at the rim, without a published neck region). More substantively, Helmholtz-type resonance for a cavity of volume V ≈ 2π ∫₀ᵃ H(r) r dr ≈ 2 × 10¹⁹ m³ with any physically motivated neck geometry does not land near 7.83 Hz either; the frequency scales as c/(2π) · √(A / (V L)) and requires fine-tuning of the neck to reach 7.83 Hz, which the dome has never performed or published. In any case, the dome’s published mechanism is quarter-wave (WIN-029), not Helmholtz, so a retreat to Helmholtz-resonance would require rewriting the model’s own derivation.

anticipated_objections.objection: If the parenthetical concedes too much to the coupled-eigenvalue objection, it weakens the paragraph’s rhetorical force — the reader may come away thinking "well, they haven’t actually computed the correct eigenvalue problem either, so there’s some residual uncertainty."
anticipated_objections.response: The concession is bounded: we grant that the honest calculation IS the coupled problem, but we show that two independent arguments — the radial-boundary globe-fingerprint and the domain-containment lower bound — already close the escape without needing to compute the coupled spectrum. Both arguments are rigorous (AE-projection identity is exact; Courant–Faber–Krahn is a theorem). The residual uncertainty the reader might feel is answered by a single empirical fact stated in the final sentence: "The dome has not computed or published any coupled-eigenvalue result that lands at 7.83 Hz." The burden of proof is on the dome to produce the coupled result, not on us to rule out every conceivable spectrum shape.

kernel_of_truth: The coupled radial-vertical eigenvalue problem on the dome-specific H(r) really IS the honest calculation for a disc cavity with a curved upper conducting boundary, and a physics-sophisticated defender’s instinct to reach for it is correct. Our flat-cylinder Bessel-mode calculation is a first-pass simplification, not the true spectrum of the dome-published domain. The patch acknowledges this directly ("Granted — "); it does not dismiss the defender’s objection. What the patch then shows is that even the honest calculation cannot save WIN-038, because (a) the radial boundary a = 20,015 km is still π · R_Earth by construction (so the globe fingerprint survives any vertical-structure refinement), and (b) the dome cavity is a strict subset of the flat-cylinder domain, so by Dirichlet-eigenvalue monotonicity under domain inclusion (Courant–Faber–Krahn), the fundamental TM-like mode of the dome cavity is at least 5.73 Hz — strictly above 7.83 Hz will never be reached, and in fact the lower bound leaves 7.83 Hz further from any allowed mode, not closer to it.

cross_win_consistency.affects_other_wins: None. The patch is confined to WIN-038.detail_evidence paragraph 4. WIN-029 and WIN-002 are referenced in neighboring paragraphs but not modified. The AE-projection identity disc_radius = π · R_Earth and the dome’s own H(r) formula are quoted, not redefined.
cross_win_consistency.relation_to_exp204: EXP-211 is a surgical follow-up to EXP-204. It does not re-open or re-litigate any EXP-204 text; the entire EXP-204 paragraph is preserved byte-for-byte except for the inserted parenthetical. If the decider prefers to regard EXP-211 as a revision of EXP-204 rather than a new expansion, the effect on the published paragraph is identical.

tldr_update_reason: TLDR remains accurate: "quarter-wave cavity minimum is 8.78 Hz, below which 7.83 Hz cannot be produced; stability is ionospheric physics." The parenthetical strengthens an already-detail-level argument (the preemptive paragraph behind <details>) and does not change the paragraph’s thrust or the WIN’s verdict (Self-Contradicted).

defense_neutralization.notes: Curmudgeon c6 rated the section minor_edit with three moderate holes remaining; this patch addresses hole 1 (in-line coupled-eigenvalue engagement). Holes 2 (heuristic-retreat scorecard quotation) and 3 (open-rim wording) are explicitly deferred as out-of-scope for ISS-1341; they may be opened as separate tickets. Analyst estimate post-patch: defense_survives ≤ 1 for the coupled-eigenvalue escape (the remaining risk is an unpublished coupled-spectrum calculation producing 7.83 Hz by fine-tuned coincidence, which is answered by the final sentence of the parenthetical and by the domain-containment lower bound).
